blocks.text: Tests First
by Dan Fakkeldy
Test-driven development in Swift, taught through a real audiobook app
Chapter 0 - The Fear of Changing Code

Picture this. It's late, the house is quiet, and you've got one small change to make. Just one. There's a little piece of shared logic in your app, something that decides how a chapter's progress gets saved, and you need to nudge it. Five minutes of work, maybe less. You make the edit. It looks right. It compiles. And then this cold feeling settles in your stomach, the one every developer knows, the one nobody warns you about when you're starting out.
You have no idea if you just broke something.

Not the part you were looking at. That part is fine, you can see it's fine. It's everything else. Because that little piece of logic you touched isn't only used by the screen in front of you. It's used by the Watch app on your wrist. It's used by the Widget sitting on the home screen. It's used by the Mac version running on a completely different machine. And right now, tonight, you can see exactly one of those things working. The other three are sitting in the dark, and any one of them might have just quietly gone wrong, and you wouldn't find out until a stranger left a one-star review describing a bug you can't even reproduce.

So what do you actually do, in that moment? Be honest. You poke at it. You open the app, you tap around the screen you changed, you watch it for a few seconds, and it seems okay, so you tell yourself it's probably fine. Then you spend the next hour with a faint hum of anxiety in the back of your skull, because "probably fine" is the best you've got. That's not engineering. That's hoping. Think about how absurd that really is. You wouldn't accept it anywhere else in your life. Imagine a chef who changes one ingredient in a sauce and then refuses to taste it, just plates it up and sends it out, hoping. Imagine an electrician who rewires one outlet and decides not to bother flipping the breaker back on to check, because checking takes time. You'd never. And yet that's exactly what poking-and-hoping is. You changed something, and instead of checking, you crossed your fingers, because actually checking everything by hand would take the rest of the night.

And the worst part is that the hoping gets heavier over time. The more your app can do, the more places that one little edit can reach, and the more terrifying it becomes to touch anything at all. There's a name for what happens next, even if nobody says it out loud. You start avoiding certain parts of your own code. There's that one tangled area you wrote six months ago, and it works, and you have decided, quietly, that you are simply never going to touch it again, because you don't trust yourself to change it without setting something on fire. So you build around it. You add little detours. The thing you were proud of becomes a place you tiptoe past. People think a codebase rots because of bad code. It doesn't. It rots because everyone's too scared to change the code they have. Fear is the actual rot. The bad code is just what fear leaves behind.

I want to name the real problem underneath that fear, because once you see it clearly, the rest of this book is just the cure, taught one idea at a time. The real problem is this: a human being cannot hold a whole app in their head. Not a real one. Not all of it at once. Your brain is wonderful, but it has a tiny workbench, and a modern app has thousands of moving parts that all quietly depend on each other in ways you stopped consciously tracking months ago. You remember maybe a dozen of those connections on a good day. The app remembers none of them for you. So when you change one shared thing, you are, by definition, changing things you can no longer see, and no amount of being smart or careful fixes that, because it isn't a problem of effort. It's a problem of memory.

And it's worth sitting with that for a second, because the instinct, when you feel the fear, is to think the answer is to try harder. To be more careful. To concentrate. But careful is not the cure, and here's why. Think of your app as a vast wall of dominoes, thousands of them, arranged in patterns that loop back on each other. Your change tips over one domino. The question that matters isn't "did I tip the right one," you can see that part. The question is "which other dominoes are now falling, three rooms away, behind a closed door." Being careful helps you tip the right domino. It does nothing at all to tell you what's clattering down in the rooms you can't see. There is no amount of staring at the one domino in front of you that reveals the ones falling out of sight. You need something standing in every room at once, watching, ready to tell you the instant anything moves. A person can't be in every room. That's not a flaw in you. It's just arithmetic. And the whole craft we're about to learn is really the craft of putting a tireless little watcher in every single room, so you never again have to wonder what fell where you weren't looking.

Let me introduce you to the example we'll keep coming back to, because it makes all of this concrete, and because it's real. There's an app called Echo. It's an open-source audiobook study player, the free-to-share kind where anyone can read the source. And here's the lovely part: it's built by one person. A working mail carrier who writes code in the spare hours around a real job, with a lot of help from an AI assistant, the same kind of help you might use. One human, building in the margins of a busy life. And this one app runs on iPhone, on Apple Watch, on the Mac, and it has a home-screen Widget too. Four faces, you might say. Four places it shows up.

Now, here's the thing that ties it straight back to that late-night dread. Echo doesn't keep four separate copies of its brain. That would be madness to maintain. Instead, all the important logic, the real thinking the app does, lives in shared folders that every version draws from. One common core, used by all of them. And that's a genuinely good decision, because it means the iPhone and the Watch and the Mac all behave the same way. They agree, because they're running the same code. But that very sameness is the trap. The instant you have one shared brain feeding four faces, a single edit to that brain can break a face you weren't even looking at. You fix something on the phone and quietly wound the Watch. The strength and the danger are the exact same fact, seen from two sides.

So how does one tired person, working alone, ever change that shared core and sleep at night? This is the whole answer, and it's the spine of everything we're going to do together. You build a second brain. A patient, tireless, slightly obsessive second brain whose entire job is to remember how every single piece of your app is supposed to behave, and to check all of it for you, in seconds, on demand. That second brain is called a test suite. A test, for now, is just a small honest example with a known answer: given this input, the app should produce that result, and a test is the little machine that feeds in the input, looks at what came out, and tells you yes, still correct, or no, something changed. Stack up enough of those tiny honest examples and you've captured, in writing, the behavior your own memory can't hold.

Let me give you the actual numbers from Echo, because they turn this from a nice idea into something you can feel the weight of. Echo's second brain is made of one hundred sixteen test files. Inside those files are five hundred eighty-six individual tests, and those tests make one thousand three hundred sixty-nine separate checks. Roughly fourteen hundred little honest questions, each with a known right answer, all of them asked and answered every time the developer runs one single command. One command. Walk away, get a coffee, come back, and you have fourteen hundred confirmations that the iPhone, the Watch, the Mac, and the Widget all still behave the way they're supposed to. That suite is not a nice-to-have. It is the only reason a solo developer can reach into the shared core, change something real, and know within minutes whether the whole thing still holds together. Take the suite away and that person is right back to poking the screen and hoping. The tests are what make the fear go away.

And I want to slow down on what that actually does to your day, because it's bigger than catching bugs. It changes the emotional shape of building software. Without the suite, every change is a small act of courage, and courage is expensive, you only have so much of it, and you run out around the time the rest of life is also asking for some. With the suite, a change stops being an act of courage and becomes an experiment. You can be reckless, in the good way. You can say, "I wonder if it'd be cleaner to do this completely differently," and just try it, knowing that if you broke something the watchers will yell at you in seconds, and if they don't yell, you're free. That freedom to experiment cheaply is, honestly, where most of the joy in programming lives, and the fear is what steals it. The suite gives it back.

On Echo, by the way, that one command runs all those tests one after another, in a single line, not all at once in parallel, because it's built on a fairly modest machine, the kind with sixteen gigabytes of memory, which is plenty for a laptop and tight for running many heavy tests at the same time. I mention this because it's a small, human detail that I want you to hold onto: this isn't some giant company with a server farm checking the work. It's one person, one ordinary computer, patiently running the watchers in a line, and that's enough. You do not need anything fancy to get the safety we're talking about. You need the watchers, and the discipline to keep them honest.

Now I owe you some honesty, right at the start, because this book would be useless if it sold you a fantasy. By the end of our time together you will be able to write a test before you write the code it's testing, and you'll understand in your bones why that's the calmest, sanest way to build software. That's the destination. But here's the truth about Echo: it was not built that way from day one. A lot of its tests were written after the bugs showed up, the painful way, where something broke, someone went hunting, found it, fixed it, and only then wrote the little test that would have caught it. That's how real software is usually made. So this book is going to teach you the disciplined version, writing the test first, because it really is better. But it's also going to teach you something just as valuable and a lot rarer: judgment. Knowing where testing genuinely pays off and where it's mostly ceremony. Because in Echo, the deep logic, the parsers that pull apart book files, the schedulers that decide what you study next, those are tested heavily, exhaustively, because that's where a mistake is invisible and expensive. The thin glue that just wires a button to the screen? Tested lightly, or barely at all. We're not building a religion here. We're building good instincts.

There's one more honest thing tucked inside that, and I want to say it plainly so it doesn't sound like a contradiction later. Writing the test after the bug, the way Echo often did it, is not failure. It's how you learn. Every single bug that ever bit that app was a lesson written in pain, and the test that got added afterward is that lesson, captured, so the same pain can never happen twice. We'll spend a whole chapter near the end on exactly this, on the moment a bug teaches you a test, because there's something almost beautiful in it. A bug, when you really look at it, is just a missing test, a behavior you cared about but never wrote down. Writing the test first is how you avoid the lesson. Writing it after is how you make sure you only have to learn the lesson once. Both are good. The first is calmer. You'll come to feel the difference yourself.

There are three ideas I want you to start hearing like background music. I won't hammer them. They'll just keep showing up, chapter after chapter, until they feel obvious. The first one you've already met: a test is a small honest example with a known answer, and writing that example first, before the code, quietly turns it from a chore into a design tool, because to write the example you have to decide what the answer should be, and deciding what the answer should be is most of the hard thinking. Most bad code in the world isn't bad because the typing was bad. It's bad because nobody decided clearly what it was supposed to do before they wrote it. The test, written first, forces that decision out of your head and onto the page, where you can look at it. The second idea is determinism, which is a fancy word for one simple thing: the test must give the exact same answer every single time you run it, today, tomorrow, on any machine, or it's worthless. A test that passes on Tuesday and fails on Wednesday for no reason isn't a second brain, it's a liar, and a liar in your test suite is worse than no test at all, because you learn to ignore it, and then one day it was telling the truth and you didn't listen. So we'll spend real effort learning how to freeze the clock, so a test about "what happens today" doesn't change its mind at midnight, and how to fence off the data so two tests can't trip over each other, and how to fake the slow and expensive parts so the answer never depends on the weather or the network or some far-off server having a bad morning. Determinism really is the whole game. And the third idea is the quiet one, the one that surprises people the first time they feel it: testable means well-designed. You'll be trying to write a test, and you'll find the code is almost impossible to test, all tangled up and reaching out to a dozen other things, and your first thought will be that testing is annoying. But it's not the testing that's annoying. The test is just the first honest user of your code, and it's telling you the truth: this thing is too tangled. The very pressure of making your code easy to test is the same pressure that makes it small, and clearly named, and broken into pieces you can hold one at a time, and easy to change without fear. Good design and testability turn out to be the same thing wearing two different hats. When you fix the code so the test gets easy, you didn't just make a test possible. You made the code better. Every time.

A few words about how this ride is going to feel, so you know what you're in for. I will never read you code. Not a single line, not a single symbol. This is meant for your ears, and code read aloud is a special kind of torture, so when there's a real piece of Echo I want you to meet, I'll just tell you what it's called in plain spoken words, the part that handles playback, the service that talks to the database, and then I'll explain the idea behind it in ordinary English. You'll come away understanding what these things do without ever wrestling with how they're spelled. And I'm going to lean hard on the why behind every practice, because the how, the actual finger-movements, becomes muscle memory fast once the why is solid, and stays a mystery forever if it isn't. That's the trap most beginner material falls into, by the way. It shows you the steps without the reasons, so you can copy them but you can't ever judge when to bend them, and judgment is the entire difference between someone following a recipe and someone who can actually cook. Every single thing we talk about will be grounded in something that genuinely happened in this one little app's life, the real bugs, the real shared-core scares, the real reason the developer set up a dedicated reviewing step whose only job is to catch exactly the cross-platform breakage we talked about, the kind where you fix the phone and wound the Watch. Think about what that step even is. It's an admission, baked right into how the project works, that the danger we opened this chapter with is so real and so constant that it deserves its own guard at the gate, a step that exists for nothing but standing between a well-meaning edit and a silently broken device. That guard and the test suite are cousins. They're both the same instinct made concrete: don't trust your memory, don't trust "probably fine," build something outside yourself that checks, every time, without getting tired. And the person I'm picturing the whole way through is you: someone building something real in the cracks of a full life, who can't afford a fragile codebase, because every hour you spend afraid to change your own work is an hour you didn't get to spend making it better.

So here's what I want you to carry out of this first chapter and into the next one. The goal was never testing for its own sake. Nobody loves tests. The goal is to be able to change your own software without that cold feeling in your stomach, to reach into the shared heart of the thing you've built and know, not hope, that everything still works. That fearlessness is the real prize, and it isn't a personality trait you're born with or a talent you lack. It's something you build, one small honest example at a time. Let's go build it.
Chapter 1 - What a Test Actually Is

Picture a vending machine. You press the button for the salted crisps, you hear the coil turn, and the salted crisps drop into the tray. You press it again tomorrow, in a different mood, on a rainy day, and the same crisps drop. The machine does not check the calendar. It does not care how you slept. Same button, same snack, every single time. Hold onto that machine, because it is going to do a lot of quiet work for us in this chapter.

Now, the word "test." It carries a strange amount of baggage. People hear it and imagine something official and intimidating — a department somewhere, a wall of red and green lights, a thing that only big companies with big budgets bother with. Or they imagine it's a chore, a tax you pay after the real work is done, the broccoli you choke down because someone told you it was good for you. I want to strip all of that away in the next few minutes, because almost none of it is true, and the part that is true is much gentler than it sounds.

Here is what a test actually is. A test is a tiny example with a known answer, written down in a way a machine can re-check, forever, without getting bored. That's it. That is the whole idea. You already do the human version of this constantly. When you learned to add, someone showed you that two plus two is four, and you checked it on your fingers, and you nodded. Two plus two is four. That's an example with a known answer. An automated test is just that same little nod — except you write it down once, and from then on the computer does the checking for you, on every change, in under a second, for the rest of the project's life. You never have to remember to check it again. The machine remembers.

And that word "automated" is worth pausing on, because it's where the real magic lives. A manual check is something a human does once and then forgets. You try the app, it seems fine, you move on. An automated test is that same check made permanent and tireless. It doesn't get tired on a Friday afternoon. It doesn't assume that because the app worked yesterday it must still work today. It re-asks its one small question every single time anyone touches the code, which is exactly the moment things tend to break and exactly the moment a human is least likely to think to look. You are, in effect, hiring an infinitely patient assistant whose only job is to keep asking the questions you'd otherwise forget to ask.

Let me make this real, because abstractions float away and concrete things stick. Throughout this book we're going to lean on one real app as our worked example. It's called Echo. It's an open-source audiobook study player — open-source meaning the code is out in the open for anyone to read and learn from — and it was built by one person, a working mail carrier, in spare hours, with a lot of help from AI. It runs on the iPhone, the Apple Watch, and the Mac, and there's a little home-screen widget too, and they all share the same core logic underneath so every device behaves the same way. I tell you who built it because I want you to feel that this is not some untouchable corporate fortress of engineering. It's one curious person, learning as they went, exactly like you're about to.

Inside Echo there's a small, self-contained piece of decision-making I love, because it's perfect for our first example. Imagine you're studying a hard passage and you've set the app to loop — to replay a stretch of audio over and over so it sinks in, the way you'd rewind a tricky bar of music. To do that, the app needs to keep answering one little question, over and over, as the audio plays: have we reached the end of the loop yet — time to jump back to the start — or do we just keep playing? There's a small piece of code whose entire job is to answer that question. In the app it has a name; spoken plainly, it's the part called Mac Bookmark Loop Decision. Don't worry about the name. Worry about the job. You hand it some numbers, and it hands you back a decision.

So picture the numbers. You've dropped three bookmarks in this chapter — markers at ten seconds, at one hundred and twenty seconds, and at three hundred seconds. Those three markers carve the audio into loopable stretches; between the first and the second is one stretch, between the second and the third is another. Right now the playhead — the little cursor showing where you are in the audio — is sitting at fifty seconds. You're partway through that first stretch. And you're playing at normal speed. Given all of that, what's the correct decision? It's: do nothing. Keep playing. You haven't reached the end of the loop yet, so there's nothing to jump back to. Just let it roll.

I want to slow down on that "do nothing," because it's more interesting than it looks. When a piece of code has genuinely nothing to do, it has to say so in some clear way — it can't just shrug. The way this particular function says "nothing to do" is by handing back an empty answer. Programmers have a tidy idea for this: a result that might be a real value or might be deliberately absent, and here it's the absent kind — an answer that is, on purpose, no answer. That's not a mistake or a blank where a number should be. It's a meaningful, intentional "there is nothing to do right now, carry on." Holding that distinction in your head — between an answer that's missing because something went wrong and an answer that's absent because absence is the correct response — will pay off again and again as you go.

And right there — that sentence I said a moment ago — that sentence is a test. Given the playhead is at fifty seconds, given bookmarks at ten and one-twenty and three hundred, given normal speed, the right answer is "do nothing, keep playing." A test takes exactly that — the setup, and the one correct answer you expect — and writes it down so the machine can confirm it. You're not testing the whole app. You're not testing the universe. You're pinning down one honest little example and saying: this, specifically, should come out this way. If you can say a sentence like that about your code, you can write a test. The writing-it-down is the easy part. The thinking — knowing what the right answer should be — that's the real work, and you just did it in your head without breaking a sweat.

Now, that same little function has a second sentence it can tell the truth about, and it's worth seeing, because it shows you the other half of its job. Keep the same three bookmarks. But this time imagine the playhead has crept right up to the edge of the first loop — you're about to spill past the next bookmark and out of the stretch you meant to repeat. Now "do nothing" would be wrong. The correct decision is to jump back. So the function hands you a concrete instruction instead of an empty one: seek back to ten-point-oh-five seconds — a hair past the ten-second mark, right at the top of the loop, ready to play the stretch again. That's a second test, the mirror image of the first. One example proves the function knows when to stay quiet; the other proves it knows when to act, and exactly where to send you. Two small sentences, two known answers, and between them they pin down the whole behaviour you cared about.

Now I want to come back to that vending machine, because it explains why this particular piece of Echo is so wonderfully easy to test. The loop decision is what programmers call a pure function. A function, loosely, is just a little machine that takes something in and gives something back. And "pure" means it behaves exactly like our vending machine: same input, same output, every single time, with no peeking. It doesn't glance at the real clock. It doesn't check whether you're connected to the internet. It doesn't reach out and change anything else in the world. You hand it the playhead position and the bookmarks and the speed — just numbers — and it hands you back a decision based only on those numbers. Nothing hidden. Nothing it sneaks off to consult behind your back.

And that purity is the whole reason testing it is a joy rather than a headache. Because the answer depends only on what you hand it, you can hand it exactly the situation you care about, and you'll get exactly the same answer every time you ask. Fifty seconds, those three bookmarks, normal speed — "keep playing." Run it now, run it tomorrow, run it on a different Mac, on a different morning — "keep playing," "keep playing," "keep playing." There's nothing to make it wobble. Compare that to a function that secretly checks the real clock: to test it you'd have to somehow freeze time, or wait for the right moment, or fake the calendar — and now your simple little check has grown teeth. The pure version asks nothing of you but the numbers. So, quietly, file this thought away, because it comes back again and again in this book: the things that are easy to test tend to be the things that were well-built in the first place. A pure function — small, honest, depending only on what you give it — is both easy to test and easy to trust. Testability and good design turn out to be the same thing wearing two different hats. We'll keep pulling on that thread.

So we've got our example and our expected answer. There's one more piece that turns it into a real, running test, and it has a slightly fancy name: the assertion. Don't let the word scare you. An assertion is simply the line in the test that compares the answer your code actually produced against the answer you expected, and then judges whether they match. That's all. You expected "keep playing." You ask the loop decision what it thinks. It says "keep playing." The assertion looks at those two side by side, sees that they agree, and — this is the lovely part — says nothing. It just passes, silently. In testing, no news is good news. A green, silent run means every example you've written down still tells the truth.

But suppose someone changes the code — maybe they're adding a new feature, maybe they fat-finger a number — and now, at fifty seconds, the loop decision wrongly says "jump back to the start." The assertion sees that the real answer and the expected answer disagree, and it does the opposite of staying quiet. It fails, loudly, immediately. And here's the kindness in it: a good failure doesn't just say "something's wrong somewhere, good luck." It tells you which file the test lives in, which exact check disagreed, and — because the person who wrote it left a human message — a sentence in plain English about what was supposed to be true. It's a smoke alarm that doesn't just shriek but actually names the room. "The kitchen. The loop decision should have kept playing at fifty seconds, and it didn't." You go straight there. You don't search the whole house.

Now let me show you something that surprises a lot of beginners, because they assume tests are only for arithmetic — for two plus two is four, for sums that have one cold mathematical answer. A test can just as easily pin down a judgement. Echo has another small piece of code, this one part of the section called the Chapter Title Matcher, and its job is to look at two titles and decide how similar they are — how confident it should be that they refer to the same chapter of a book. The function, spoken plainly, is just called similarity. Now, "how similar are these two titles" is not a crisp arithmetic fact. It's a fuzzy, human kind of judgement. And yet you can still test it, because you can still say what the right answer ought to be.

Watch. Feed it the title "Chapter Two" and the title "Chapter One." What should the answer be? These are plainly different chapters — close in wording, sure, but anyone can see they're not the same thing — so the expected similarity should be very low. In the real app, it scores below one tenth, way down near the bottom of the scale, which is exactly right: barely similar at all. Now feed it a different pair. Give it a full, fleshed-out title — something like "Chapter Three, How to Stop Making Things Worse" — and pit it against a bare heading that just reads "How to Stop Making Things Worse." A human would glance at those and go, "well, obviously, that's the same chapter, one just has the number bolted on." And the function agrees: it scores that pair high, comfortably above the bar Echo uses to say "yes, confident, these match." So you write two tests. The first says different chapters should score low. The second says the same chapter, dressed differently, should score high. Each test is one human intention — one thing you meant — turned into something the machine will stand guard over from now on. The fuzziness lived in building the function. The test only needs you to know what a good answer looks like, and you do.

And notice what those two title tests are really doing together. One picks a case that should clearly fail to match, and checks it stays low. The other picks a case that should clearly match, and checks it climbs high. You're not asking the machine to define "similar" for you — you'll never get a perfect, philosophical definition of that, and you don't need one. You're staking out two honest fence posts, a definite "no" and a definite "yes," and trusting the function to behave sensibly between them. That's a quietly powerful move. Even when the underlying idea is fuzzy, you can almost always name a few examples where the answer is obvious, and obvious examples make perfectly good tests.

There's one more gift hiding in all of this, and it might be my favourite, so let me end on it. A test is documentation that cannot lie. Think about the usual ways we describe what code does. We write a comment — a little note in the margin of the code — explaining what some piece is meant to do. We write a read-me file describing how the app behaves. Both are helpful, and both share a fatal flaw: they're just words sitting next to the code, and words drift. Someone changes the code in a hurry and forgets to update the comment. The behaviour described in the read-me quietly stops being true, and nobody notices for six months. The documentation rots, silently, while everyone keeps trusting it.

A test cannot do that. A test isn't a description sitting next to the code; it's a description that gets run against the code, every single time, automatically. The moment the code stops matching what the test says, the test fails and tells you. It physically cannot drift out of sync without screaming about it. Which means a passing test is the one form of documentation in the whole project that is forced, on pain of going red, to stay honest. When you read a test that says "at fifty seconds with these bookmarks, keep playing," you're not reading someone's possibly-outdated hope about the code. You're reading a fact the machine reconfirmed seconds ago.

And Echo has a lot of these facts. Its automated test suite is spread across a hundred and sixteen separate test files, holding five hundred and eighty-six individual tests between them, which together make one thousand three hundred and sixty-nine assertions — that is, one thousand three hundred and sixty-nine little side-by-side comparisons of what the code did against what it ought to do, all re-run with a single command. Most of those files — a hundred and seven of them — are written with the newer, friendlier testing framework, and only a handful still lean on the older one, which tells you something about how the project has been steadily modernising as it grows. But the number to hold onto isn't really any of those totals. It's the idea behind them: five hundred and eighty-six small, honest sentences about how the app behaves, none of them allowed to lie, all of them re-checked the instant anyone changes a thing. That is a remarkable safety net for one person in spare hours to have built around themselves — and it started exactly where you're standing now, with one tiny example and one known answer.

So here's what to carry forward. A test is not a ceremony and it's not a chore. It's a small, honest example with an answer you already know, written down so a machine can re-check it forever. Once you really see it that way, the fear drains out of the word, and something better takes its place. Because the next thing we're going to do feels backwards the very first time, and then becomes second nature faster than you'd believe: we're going to write that little example before we write the code it describes — and watch how, the moment you do, the test stops being a check on your work and quietly becomes the thing that designs it.
Chapter 2 - Red, Green, Refactor

Picture this. You sit down to add a new behavior to your app. You know roughly what you want it to do. And the very first thing you type is not the behavior. It's a test for the behavior. A small, honest little example that checks the answer the behavior is supposed to give. There's just one problem. The behavior doesn't exist yet. There is no code for the test to call. So when you run that test, it can't possibly pass. It fails. And here is the strange part: you knew it would fail. You wrote it on purpose, ran it on purpose, and watched it go red on purpose.

Sit with how backwards that feels for a second. It's like writing the answer key to a quiz before you've written the quiz. It's like building the frame for a picture you haven't painted. Every instinct says, surely you write the thing first, and then, if you have time and energy left over, you check it. Writing the check first feels like buttoning your coat before you've put it on.

But it is, quietly, one of the most brilliant moves in all of software, and once it clicks you'll never quite see coding the same way. Because that little failing test is not a chore you're doing to yourself. It's a target. You've just written down, in the most concrete way possible, exactly what "done" looks like for the next tiny step. Not a vague feeling of done. A precise, runnable answer that the machine can check for you. And the moment that test goes from red to green, you will know, not hope, not assume, but know, that this one small thing now works.

There's something else hiding in that move, too, something you only feel after you've done it a few times. When you write the test first, you are forced to use the thing before you build it. You have to decide, right there, what you're going to hand it and what you expect back. You become its first customer before you become its builder. And being a customer is clarifying in a way that being a builder is not, because as a customer you only care about what's convenient and obvious, not about the messy insides. More often than you'd guess, you'll write that first little example and feel a tug of, "ugh, that's awkward to call," and you'll change the shape of the thing before it even exists, purely because using it felt clumsy. That tug is the test designing your code for you. We'll come back to that idea again and again, that the pressure to make something testable is the very same pressure that makes it small, and clearly named, and pleasant to use. For now, just notice that you got a design opinion for free, before you'd written one line of the real thing.

That move has a rhythm, and the rhythm has a name. It's called red, green, refactor. Three beats, repeated over and over, like a heartbeat under everything you build. Let's take them one at a time, because each beat is doing a job, and the job matters.

The first beat is red. You write a small test that describes the very next behavior you want. Just one. You run it, and you watch it fail. Now, you might think failing is the bad outcome here, the thing to avoid. It's the opposite. Watching the test fail is a gift, because it proves the test can fail. Think about what that means. A test that fails when the behavior is missing is a test that's actually wired up to reality, actually looking at the thing it claims to look at. If you skipped this step, and wrote your code and your test together, and the test passed on the first try, you'd have no idea whether it passed because your code is correct or because the test isn't really checking anything at all. A test that passes before you've written a single line of the real code is a test that's testing nothing. It's a smoke detector with no battery in it. It will sit there, green and reassuring, and it will never once warn you of a fire. So you make it go red first, deliberately, just to see the light come on. That red is the test saying, "I'm awake, I'm watching, and right now the answer is wrong." Good. Now you trust it.

The second beat is green. With your failing test glaring at you, you write the code. And here's the discipline, the part that's genuinely hard for clever people: you write the simplest possible code that makes that one test pass. Nothing more. Not the whole feature. Not the elegant general solution you can already picture. Not the seven other cases you know are coming. Just enough, even something a little crude, a little dumb, to turn that one red light green.

This is liberating once you let it be. You're allowed to be dumb here. If the test asks, "what's two plus two," you are permitted to write code that simply answers four, full stop, and move on. It feels like cheating. It isn't. The test is the spec, the whole spec, the only spec, and your single job in this beat is to satisfy it. The reason this works is that you're not trying to be right about the future. You're trying to be right about right now, about this one example, and the future will arrive one test at a time and tell you what it needs.

I want to defend the dumbness a little harder, because it's the part people resist most. When you sit down to build a feature, your head is full of the whole thing at once. You can see the elegant general version, the one that handles every case gracefully, and you want to type all of it right now while it's vivid. But that instinct is exactly how code goes wrong. You build the grand general machine, and three of its seven cleverly handled cases turn out to be cases that never actually happen, and two of them are subtly wrong in ways you won't discover for weeks, and the whole thing is now too big to hold in your head, so you're afraid to touch it. Writing the simplest dumb thing that passes the one test in front of you is a way of refusing to get ahead of your own evidence. You only build what an example has demanded. If no test has asked for a case, that case does not exist yet, and you do not write code for it. The feature stays exactly as smart as it needs to be and not one bit smarter, which, it turns out, is the right amount of smart.

When that test flips to green, you get a small hit of pure relief, and I don't want to undersell that, because it's part of what keeps you going. The red was a small, honest accusation: this doesn't work yet. The green is the accusation withdrawn. The thing you described now actually happens, demonstrably, in front of you, and the machine just confirmed it. You earned that, and it took almost no code to get there, and you can feel your shoulders drop a half inch. Do this loop for an afternoon and you start to crave the green. It becomes a little engine of momentum, one small honest win clicking into the next.

The third beat is refactor, and this is where the magic compounds. To refactor means to change how the code is written without changing what it does. That's the whole definition, and it's worth saying slowly: same behavior, better shape. You rename a thing so its purpose is obvious. You pull a tangled line apart into two clear ones. You notice you've written nearly the same few lines twice and you fold them into one. You make the code read like a sentence instead of a riddle. And the entire time you're doing this, you have a safety net stretched out beneath you, because your test is still there, still green, still watching. So the moment you finish tidying, you run it again. If it's still green, you have proof, real proof, that you changed how the code reads without changing what it does. You tidied bravely, and nothing broke, and you know nothing broke because the net held. Without that net, cleaning up working code is terrifying, and most people simply don't do it. They leave the mess because touching it feels like defusing a bomb. With the net, tidying becomes safe, almost casual. Red, green, refactor. Describe the next step, prove it works, clean it up. Then around again.

It's worth being clear about what refactoring is not, because the word gets thrown around loosely. Refactoring is not adding a feature. It is not fixing a bug. It is not making the code do anything new at all. The instant the behavior changes, you've stepped out of refactoring and into something else, and your green test should notice and complain. That's the whole point of doing it under a passing test: the test is the definition of "what it does," held fixed, while you reshape "how it's written" around it. If you reshape and the test stays green, you stayed honest. If you reshape and the test goes red, you didn't refactor, you broke something, and you found out in seconds instead of in a bug report. The net doesn't just make you brave. It makes you accurate about what you actually did.

Let me make this concrete with a real piece of Echo, because the loop reads most clearly when you can watch it grow something line by line. Echo is an audiobook study player, and one of its small, fiddly features is a loop. You can drop two bookmarks, and when playback reaches the second one it jumps back to the first, so a tricky passage repeats itself for you, over and over, hands-free, while you're out walking your route. There's a small piece of logic at the heart of that, a single decision-maker. You hand it three things: the current moment in the audio, the list of bookmark times, and how fast you're playing. And it hands back one of two answers: either a place to jump back to, or nothing at all, meaning carry on, don't loop.

Now watch how that little decision-maker reads like something that grew one test at a time, because it almost certainly did. You start with the very simplest rule you can think of. With fewer than two bookmarks, there's nothing to loop between, so never loop. You write a test for exactly that: one bookmark, or none, and the answer should be nothing. Red. Then you write the smallest code that satisfies it. Green. One branch of the logic now exists, the simplest one, and it's pinned down forever.

Next rule. Suppose you've got two bookmarks and you're playing somewhere comfortably in the middle of the stretch between them, nowhere near the far end yet. The answer should be nothing. Keep playing. So you write that test. It fails, because your code so far only knows the no-bookmarks case. Red. You add just enough to handle being mid-segment. Green. Another branch, born from another test.

Next rule, and this is the heart of it. As playback creeps up close to that second bookmark, within about half a second of it at normal speed, now it should fire. The answer flips from nothing to a place to jump back to: the start of the segment, the first bookmark, plus a tiny nudge forward. If the segment starts at the ten-second mark, you don't jump back to exactly ten. You jump back to ten-point-oh-five, ten seconds and a hair. You write a test that says, when I'm in that look-ahead window, give me back ten-point-oh-five. Red, then green, and now the looping branch exists too.

Then one more. Speed changes everything, because at faster playback you cover ground quicker, so the window where you need to react has to open earlier. At triple speed, that half-second look-ahead stretches to nearly a full second, because the audio is rushing past three times as fast and you'd overshoot the bookmark if you waited as long. So you write a test at triple speed, putting yourself at a position that would be safely mid-segment at normal pace but should already trigger the loop when you're moving fast. It fails, because your code doesn't yet scale the window with speed. Red. You make the window widen with the speed. Green. And feel the rhythm in that. Each new test forced exactly one new branch of the logic into being. The behavior didn't arrive in a single heroic block. It accreted, one honest example at a time, each one red before it was green, each one cleaned up before the next one started.

There's one more test in that little family that's easy to forget but tells you a lot about the mindset. Sometimes, for a fraction of a second while audio is loading or seeking, the current position isn't a real number at all. It's a strange not-a-number value, the kind of thing that arises when a calculation has nothing sensible to chew on yet. A decision-maker that hasn't thought about that case might do something bizarre when handed it, and the loop might lurch or misfire at exactly the wrong moment. So there's a test that pushes that not-a-number value straight in and insists the only safe answer comes back: nothing, don't loop, just carry on. That test exists because someone imagined the messy real world rather than the tidy happy path, and pinned the safe behavior down so it can't quietly rot away. And that's the texture of a real test suite. Echo's has grown to well over five hundred individual tests across more than a hundred files, with something like thirteen hundred separate little checks inside them, and the whole lot runs from a single command. Most of those tests are exactly this humble: a small honest example, an obvious case, a nasty edge case, each one once red, each one made green, each one now standing guard. They didn't arrive in a flood. They accumulated, one loop at a time, the same heartbeat repeated until it added up to a wall of safety you can lean your whole weight on.

And that tiny nudge, the ten-point-oh-five instead of a flat ten, is worth pausing on, because it shows you something tests do that's easy to miss. Why a hair past the bookmark, instead of landing exactly on it? Because if you jumped back to precisely the bookmark's position, you'd be sitting right on the trigger, and the loop might instantly fire again, and again, stuttering in place like a record skipping. The little nudge carries you just past the trip wire so the next loop has room to breathe. That's a subtle, almost invisible bit of intent. And there's a test whose entire job is to pin that intent down, to assert that the answer really is ten-point-oh-five and not a flat ten. Why bother? Because months from now, someone, maybe you, will be tidying this code and think, that funny little point-oh-five looks like a mistake, let me clean it up to a nice round number. And the instant they do, that test goes red and says, no, stop, that nudge is on purpose, here's why. The test remembers the reason long after the person who wrote it has forgotten. A bug that would have crept right back in gets caught at the door.

Now, let me be honest with you, because this loop is not free and I'd be doing you a disservice to pretend it is. Writing the test first genuinely feels slower at the very start of a task. You're typing checks for code that doesn't exist while a voice in your head insists you could've just written the feature by now. And the payback is real but it's spread out across the whole life of the code, every future change you make without fear, every bug the tests catch before your users do. That's a hard trade to feel in the moment. The slowness is concrete and immediate; the payoff is diffuse and later. You have to take a little of it on faith at first, until you've lived through enough "thank goodness that test was there" moments to believe it in your bones.

And there's a specific situation where test-first turns genuinely awkward, and I won't hide it from you. Sometimes you don't yet know the shape of the answer. You can't write a clean test for a behavior you can't yet describe, and you can't describe it because you're still figuring out, fundamentally, how the thing should even work. Forcing yourself to write tests in that fog is like trying to write the answer key for a quiz in a subject you haven't learned. So here's the pragmatic escape hatch, and it's completely allowed. You go off and do a throwaway exploration, sometimes called a spike. You write rough, messy, untested code, fast and loose, purely to learn the shape of the problem. You poke at it until the fog lifts and you understand what the answer actually wants to look like. And then, and this is the discipline, you throw that exploration away. You delete it. You come back to a clean slate, and now that you know the shape, you write the tests properly, one at a time, red, green, refactor, building the real thing for keeps. The spike was a scouting trip, not the house you live in.

So that's the heartbeat. Red, green, refactor. Write a small, failing test that names the next behavior. Make it pass with the least code you can get away with. Then clean up what you wrote, safe in the net of a passing test, and run it again to prove you only changed the shape. Carry this with you, because it's the rhythm running underneath everything else we're going to do in this book. Tiny step, prove it, clean it, repeat. Next, we'll get our hands on the actual modern tools Swift gives you to write that very first failing test, and you'll see how little ceremony it really takes to make a light turn red.
Chapter 3 - Writing Tests in Swift

So here you are, convinced. You've decided you're going to write the failing test first, before you write a single line of the thing it tests. You've heard the promise: that a test is just a small, honest example with a known answer, and that writing it first turns it from a chore into a quiet little design tool. You believe it. And then you sit down, and your hands hover over the keyboard, and a very practical, very honest question arrives. What do you actually type?

That's the right question to be asking. It's also the one most books skip past in a hurry, as if the typing were obvious. It isn't obvious, not the first time. There's a particular flavor of stage fright that hits right here — you understand the idea perfectly, you could explain it to a friend over coffee, and yet faced with a blank file you freeze, because thinking about a test and actually shaping one with your fingers feel like two different skills. They are two different skills. The good news is that the second one is far smaller than it looks.

So I want to set your mind at ease before we go one step further. The modern tools for writing tests in Swift read almost like plain English. They were deliberately designed that way — somebody at Apple clearly sat down and asked, "what if writing a test felt less like operating a machine and more like making a plain statement out loud?" When you write a test with these tools, you're not really wrestling with machinery. You're writing a sentence that says, in effect, "I expect this to equal that." And here's a small mercy I'll promise you up front: you will never, in this whole book, have to read any of that aloud, symbol by symbol, the way a computer would. I'll always translate it into the spoken shape of the idea, into the meaning under the punctuation. So relax your shoulders. You're learning a way of thinking, not memorizing keystrokes.

Let me introduce you to the framework our example app, Echo, uses for nearly all of its tests. It has a refreshingly literal name. People say it out loud as "Swift Testing." It comes from Apple, and it arrived in 2024, which makes it the new kid, the modern option. A framework, by the way, is just a toolkit somebody else built so you don't have to invent the plumbing yourself. This particular toolkit gives you a handful of plain-English ideas, and honestly, three of them carry most of the weight.

The first idea is how you say, "this little function is a test." In ordinary code, a function is just a named recipe — a set of steps you can run. To turn one of those recipes into a test, you put a small tag on it, a little label the framework recognizes. Out loud, people call that tag "at-Test." You don't need to picture the symbols. Just hold the meaning: you take a perfectly ordinary function, you pin a tag to it that says "this one is a test," and now the toolkit knows to run it and check the result. That's all. A test is not some exotic species of code. It's a normal function wearing a name badge.

The second idea is how you state what you expect. This is the actual heart of the thing. You write a check — out loud, people call it "expect" — and you hand it a claim. Expect that this equals that. Expect that the list has three items. Expect that the trimmed title is no longer blank. If the claim is true, the test sails on, silent and content. If the claim is false, the test fails, loudly and specifically, and tells you exactly which claim broke. That's the whole transaction. You're not describing how the code works. You're stating, in advance, what a correct answer looks like, and letting the machine be the honest referee that compares your claim against reality.

The third idea is a stricter cousin of the second, and it exists for a very particular moment. Sometimes a value might be missing entirely. Maybe you asked for the first chapter of a book, and there's a real possibility — given some bug — that there is no first chapter at all, that the thing came back empty-handed. Now, if you blindly try to keep testing a chapter that isn't there, you don't get a clean failure. You can get an ugly crash that takes down the whole run, like a single tripped breaker plunging the entire house into darkness. So for exactly that situation, the toolkit gives you a sterner check. Out loud, people call it "require." Require that this value is actually present. If it's there, fine, carry on. If it's missing, this one test stops, cleanly and immediately, and reports itself as failed — without dragging every other test down with it. In Echo's suite there are fifty-three of those stricter checks, sprinkled exactly where a missing value would otherwise turn a tidy failure into a mess. Think of "expect" as your everyday referee, and "require" as the one you call in when, if the claim is false, there's simply no point continuing.

Now, where do all these little test functions live? They don't float around loose. Related tests get gathered together into a group the framework calls a suite — and a suite, beautifully, is usually nothing more exotic than a plain Swift structure. A structure is just a simple container that holds some related things together; here, it holds your test functions. So a suite is a tidy folder of related examples, sitting in one place, easy to find. Echo has twenty-five of these named suites, each one a little neighborhood of tests that belong together — one for the part that parses books into chapters, one for the part that schedules study reviews, one for the piece that lines up the spoken audio with the written page, and so on. When something breaks, you generally know which neighborhood to walk into first, and that alone saves a surprising amount of time.

A few of Echo's suites carry one extra instruction worth a quick word, because it connects to something deeper than it first appears. Some of them are tagged to run on what people call "the main thread" — out loud, you'd hear it spoken as "at Main Actor." Don't let the name worry you. The idea is simply this: certain parts of an app, especially anything touching the screen or the local database, are fussy about being handled on one specific lane of traffic, the main one, rather than on any old side street. So the tests for those parts are politely told, "run yourself on that main lane." It's a small label that keeps delicate tests on the same road the real app uses, so the test conditions match the live ones. You don't need to master it now. Just notice that even here, the framework is letting you state your intentions in plain, declarative words rather than wiring up plumbing by hand.

But here is the detail I want you to actually fall a little bit in love with, because it quietly does more good than almost anything else in the whole framework. For every single test, the framework builds a brand-new, fresh copy of that suite. Not one shared copy that all the tests scribble on in turn — a new one, from scratch, for each test, every time. Picture a science lab where, before every experiment, somebody wheels in a completely clean desk. Fresh paper. Nothing left over from the last person. No half-finished calculation in the corner, no coffee ring, no stray note that might nudge your result. Each test sits down at its own clean desk, does its one experiment, and leaves. The next test gets its own clean desk too.

Why does that matter so much? Because it's the thing that makes your answers trustworthy. If tests shared a desk, one test could leave a value sitting around — a counter nudged up, a flag flipped, a list half-filled — and the next test might pass or fail depending on which test happened to run first, or whether one ran at all. And tests don't always run in a tidy, predictable order; the framework reserves the right to shuffle them, even run several at once. So a shared desk turns your test results into a kind of weather: technically explainable, but unpredictable in practice, and maddening to chase. That's the real nightmare — a test that's green on Tuesday and red on Wednesday for no reason you can see, that passes alone and fails in a crowd. People have lost whole afternoons to exactly that ghost. The fresh-desk rule kills it at the root, before it can ever haunt you. Nothing carries over, so nothing can poison the next result.

It's the same principle we'll keep circling back to all through this book — the idea that the world has to stand still for a test, that the answer must be identical on every single run, today, tomorrow, on your machine and on a colleague's. Isolation buys you repeatability, and repeatability is the entire reason a test is worth trusting. And there's a quieter, second gift hiding inside the fresh desk, one we'll keep noticing too: code that's easy to test in a clean little box, all by itself, with nothing left over from before, tends to be code that's been kept small and well-behaved in the first place. If a chunk of your code can't survive on a clean desk — if it only works when a dozen other things have already happened in just the right order — that's not a testing problem. That's the code quietly telling you it's tangled. So the fresh desk doesn't only keep your tests honest. It holds up a mirror to your design, and gently pressures it toward something smaller and cleaner.

So that's what the tests are. How do you actually run the things in Echo? This part is almost anticlimactic, in the best way. There's one command. People say it as "make test." You type that, you press return, and the toolkit goes off and builds the app and runs the whole suite — all of it, hundreds of tests, every named claim — and then comes back and tells you what passed and what didn't. One command, one verdict. That's the loop. That's the rhythm you're chasing.

There are a couple of small, practical wrinkles worth knowing, because they're the kind of thing nobody mentions until you hit them face-first. Echo is built on a machine with sixteen gigabytes of memory, which sounds like a generous amount until you ask it to run hundreds of tests all at the very same instant. Every test you run at once wants its own little slice of room to work in, and if you let them all crowd through the door together, the machine can choke — it slows to a crawl, or worse, falls over entirely, and then you can't even tell whether your code was fine, because the messenger collapsed before delivering the news. So Echo's guidance is deliberately modest: run the tests more or less one at a time, patiently, in a single file, rather than as a frantic mob all shoving at once. It's a touch slower in the moment, sure. But it's steady, and it never lies to you, and steady-and-honest is exactly what you want from a thing you'll run a hundred times a day. A fast tool you can't trust is worse than a slow one you can.

And there's a faster gear waiting for when you're deep in a tight back-and-forth — changing one small thing, wanting to know right now whether it worked, then changing it again. Rebuilding the entire app from scratch on every one of those little loops would be like reheating the whole oven each time you want to check on one tray. So instead you build the tests just once, up front, and then re-run a single suite by its name, over and over, against that already-built foundation. Change, check, change, check, fast enough that you barely break your train of thought. And that — right there — is the whole quiet point of every piece of this. The one command, the fresh desks, the steady serial pace, the build-once-rerun-one shortcut: all of it exists to make that feedback loop quick enough and trustworthy enough that you actually reach for it, by reflex, dozens of times a day. Because a test you wrote but never run is not protection. It's just a wish you typed out and filed away.

Now I owe you an honest comparison, because Swift Testing is not the only game in town, and pretending otherwise would do you a disservice the first time you go looking for help online. There is an older framework. People say its name as "XCTest." It is not dead, not deprecated, not going anywhere. It still ships, it's been around for many years, and it is absolutely everywhere — the overwhelming majority of test code written for Apple platforms before 2024 used it, and a great deal still does. Echo itself still keeps it around, but only in one small corner: the handful of tests that drive the actual user interface, tapping buttons and checking that the right screen appears. That's about seven files out of a hundred and sixteen. Everything else — the other hundred and seven files — Echo writes in the modern framework.

The older one feels different to work in. Where the new framework lets a suite be a plain, lightweight structure that gets freshly rebuilt for each test, the older one is built around classes — a heavier, more old-fashioned kind of container — and its checks are phrased a little more stiffly. Instead of the conversational "expect that this equals that," you write something people say out loud as "assert-equal," which is more like barking an instruction than stating a claim. It works perfectly well. Millions of solid, trustworthy tests are written exactly that way. It just reads a half-step less like a human sentence.

So why did Echo bet on the modern framework for nearly everything? Three honest reasons. First, it reads like plain English, and when you're a solo developer reading your own tests at the end of a long day, that readability is not a luxury — it's the difference between maintaining your tests and quietly abandoning them. Second, when something fails, the modern framework's messages are far more helpful; it doesn't just say "false," it shows you the actual values it compared and where the claim broke, so a failure points at its own cause instead of sending you on a hunt. And third — this is the one we'll spend a whole chapter on soon — the modern framework can take a single example and run it many times over, once for each of a whole table of different inputs, without you copying and pasting the test a dozen times. Hold that thought. It's going to matter more than you'd guess.

But I promised honesty, and honesty cuts both ways, so let me name the tradeoff squarely, because choosing the new thing is never free. The modern framework is, well, new. It needs a recent enough system and a recent enough version of Xcode — that's Apple's big workshop app where all of this gets built — so on an older setup it may simply not be available to you. And because it's young, the pile of online answers is thinner. When you get stuck at eleven at night and paste your problem into a search box, you'll find fewer people who hit exactly your wall and wrote down how they climbed it. The older framework, by sheer age and ubiquity, has an answer for almost everything, somewhere, written by somebody, years ago. That deep well of accumulated help is a real advantage, and it would be dishonest to wave it away.

So how do you weigh it? For Echo, the math came out clearly in favor of the new tools — but notice why. This was a brand-new test suite, written from scratch, where the thing that mattered most was day-to-day readability, the comfort of opening a test six months later and understanding it instantly. When that's your priority, and you're on a modern setup anyway, the modern choice pays for itself every single day. But — and I want to be very clear about this — that is a judgment, not a law of nature. On an older, established codebase, one already carrying thousands of tests in the older framework, with a team that knows it cold, you might very sensibly choose to stay put. Good engineering is full of decisions like this: not right and wrong, but right-for-this-situation. Knowing that the choice exists, and why it went the way it did here, is worth more than any rule I could hand you.

I want to end on a small, human detail, because it's the part that turns testing from a cold chore into something almost companionable. Remember that "expect" check — the one where you state your claim? You're allowed to attach a written sentence to it, in plain words, that prints out only if the claim fails. And this little touch changes everything about how a broken test feels. Echo has a real test that checks something subtle: that those generic, meaningless chapter labels — the ones that just say "Chapter" and a number, which carry no real information about what the chapter is actually about — produce no false matches when the app tries to line up audio with text. And the developer didn't just leave the check to fail with a bare, stony "false." He attached a sentence. Something to the effect of: these generic labels carry no real signal, and yet we got three matches anyway. So when that test goes red, it doesn't hand you a wall of meaningless numbers. It hands you a note, written by a thoughtful colleague — who happens to be your past self — explaining in plain English what was supposed to be true, and gently pointing at exactly how reality disagreed. A red test, done well, reads like someone leaning over your shoulder and saying, "hey, this is off, and here's what I was expecting." That's not a chore. That's a kindness you do for your future self.

And that's really what you should carry forward from here. You now have the vocabulary — the tag that marks a function as a test, the everyday check that states a claim, the stricter one that stops cleanly when a value is missing, and the fresh desk that keeps every test honest. You have enough, right now, to sit down and write that first failing test without your hands hovering uncertainly over the keys. But there's a trap waiting just ahead, one that catches almost everyone the moment their first test turns green. You write one tidy example, it passes, and a warm little voice whispers that you're done. You are not done. One passing example is nowhere near enough — and in the next chapter, we'll look at exactly why, and at the lovely trick the modern framework gives us for handling all the messy, awkward cases that one happy example politely ignored.
Chapter 4 - One Example Isn't Enough

There's a particular feeling you get the first time a test turns green. You wrote a small, honest example. You gave it an input, you stated the answer you expected, and you pressed run. And there it was: a little green checkmark, calm and reassuring, telling you the code did exactly what you said it would. You sit back. You feel safe. For a moment, you believe you're done.

You're not done. And I want to be gentle about this, because the feeling is real and it's earned — you did prove something. But here's the trap hiding inside that green checkmark. You proved the code works for one input. One friendly, well-behaved, cooperative input. The kind of input you'd hand to a friend. The kind you thought of first because it was the easiest to imagine.

The problem is that bugs don't live in the friendly inputs. Nobody's code breaks on the happy little example you dreamed up while you were feeling optimistic. Code breaks at the edges. It breaks on the empty list, the value that's one bigger than you expected, the moment right before the first thing happens and the moment right after the last thing ends. It breaks on the input that should never, ever arrive — and then arrives anyway. So a single passing test isn't a clean bill of health. It's one honest example with a known answer, and that's genuinely valuable, but it's the beginning of the conversation, not the end of it. The rest of this chapter is about learning to ask the second question, and the third, and the eighth: what else could happen here?

Let me make this concrete, because abstract advice about edge cases never sticks. Inside Echo — that's the open-source audiobook study player we've been using as our worked example, the one built by a working mail carrier in his spare hours with a little help from an AI assistant — there's a small piece of logic that decides how to loop a stretch of audio. Imagine you've set two bookmarks in a book to mark a passage you want to drill, and you want the player to loop just that segment, over and over, while you study. Something has to look at where you currently are in the audio and decide: do I keep playing, do I jump back to the start of this segment, or do I stop? That little decision-maker has eight tests sitting around it. Eight. For what sounds like simple arithmetic.

And here's the thing — the arithmetic genuinely is simple. Comparing where you are against where the bookmarks sit is not hard math. The eight tests aren't there because the calculation is complicated. They're there because the edges are where it breaks, and there turn out to be a surprising number of edges. Let me walk a few, so you can feel the texture of edge-case thinking.

Consider the very beginning, before there's anything to loop. If you've set fewer than two bookmarks, there's no segment yet — you need a start and an end to make a loop, and one lonely point doesn't make a segment. So the right answer is: do nothing. That's a test. Then think about being positioned before the very first bookmark, earlier in the audio than the segment even starts. There's nothing to loop back to yet, so again: do nothing. Another test, and notice it's a genuinely different situation from the first one, even though the answer happens to be the same. In the first case the bookmarks barely exist; in the second they exist but you haven't reached them. Same answer, different reason — and you want a test pinning down each reason, because tomorrow someone might change the logic in a way that breaks one of those two paths while leaving the other working. If you only had one test, you'd never know which path you'd quietly snapped.

Now picture the ordinary, well-behaved middle of the loop, because the edges are easier to appreciate once you've seen the calm center. You're inside the segment, playing along, and you're approaching the end bookmark — getting close to the spot where the loop should snap you back. The right answer there is to seek back to the start of the segment so you can hear it again. Simple enough. But there's a wrinkle that's easy to miss, and it's a lovely little detail about how this thing actually works in your ear. The player can't wait until you've literally hit the end bookmark to decide to jump, because by then the voice has already spoken past it — you'd hear a sliver of the next sentence before the rewind kicked in, and the loop would feel sloppy. So instead it looks ahead a little, deciding to jump slightly before the true end. And here's the part that delighted me when I understood it: how far ahead it looks depends on how fast you're listening. If you've cranked the playback up to a faster speed, the audio is rushing toward that end bookmark more quickly, so the look-ahead window has to widen to compensate — it needs to start the jump earlier in clock time to land in the right place. That's a whole separate test: faster speed, wider look-ahead. It's the kind of subtlety nobody thinks of on the happy path, and exactly the kind of thing that, left untested, produces a loop that feels fine at normal speed and oddly clipped when you speed it up.

There's even a test for what happens on the second time around — the segment, having played once, should loop back to its own start rather than wandering off somewhere else. It sounds almost too obvious to bother checking. But "obvious" is precisely the kind of assumption that rots quietly when someone refactors the code six months later, and a test standing guard over the obvious thing is a test that catches the day the obvious thing stops being true.

Now move to the far end. Say you've drifted just past the last bookmark — within about a second of it. That's close enough that you've clearly finished the segment, so the player should loop the final stretch back to its start and let you hear it again. But drift further — more than a second past the end — and the intent is different. You've left the loop behind on purpose. So now the right answer is to stop looping and just let the book play on. One second of difference flips the entire decision. That boundary, that exact threshold, is precisely the kind of place a bug loves to hide, and precisely the kind of place a single friendly test would sail right past without ever noticing. You need a test sitting just inside that one-second window and another sitting just outside it, one on each side of the line, because the whole point of a boundary is that both sides have to behave, and the only way to know they do is to check them both.
And then there's the sharp one. The nasty one. The test for a time value that isn't a number at all.

I want to dwell here, because this single case teaches the entire mindset better than anything else I could show you. In real audio playback, the position you're at — how many seconds into the track you are — is almost always a perfectly ordinary number. But "almost always" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. Occasionally, for reasons that come from deep down in the media machinery, the position arrives as a special non-number value. Not zero. Not negative. Not huge. Literally a thing the system uses to mean "this is not a number" — a placeholder for a calculation that went nowhere, like dividing by zero and getting back a shrug.

Here's why that's so dangerous, and why it's the perfect teacher. This non-number value has a genuinely strange property: any comparison you make against it comes back false. Is it greater than the bookmark? False. Is it less than the bookmark? Also false. Is it equal to the bookmark? Still false. It refuses every comparison. So if you'd written your loop logic the naive, obvious way — the way you'd write it on a good day, thinking only about the friendly inputs — you'd have a series of checks that all quietly answer false, and the bad value would slip straight through the middle of your logic like a ghost through a wall. It wouldn't trip any of your guards, because your guards were built assuming the input was a normal number that would honestly answer yes or no. And then, somewhere later — maybe seconds later, maybe in a completely different part of the app — that ghost causes a crash, or a freeze, or a loop that loops forever to nowhere. And you'll spend an afternoon hunting for the cause in entirely the wrong place, because the place where it broke is nowhere near the place where it slipped in.

So somebody had to sit down and ask the question. Not "what does this code do on a good day?" but "what is the single nastiest input this thing could ever, in its whole life, be handed?" And then they had to write the test that pins that answer down for good: when the position is not a number, the decision must be to safely do nothing — return nothing, change nothing, crash nothing. That question — what's the worst thing that could come through this door? — that question is the craft. It's not a knack you're born with. It's a habit you build, one ugly input at a time, and the loop-decision suite with its eight tests is what that habit looks like written down. Eight small examples, each one a question somebody had the discipline to ask before a real user's audio could ask it for them.

Now, you may already be feeling a small dread. If edges multiply like this, and I need a test for each one, am I doomed to write twenty nearly identical tests, each one a copy of the last with one tiny number changed? Copy, paste, tweak, copy, paste, tweak, until my test file is a wall of near-duplicates that all blur together?

No. There's a lovely tool for exactly this situation, and it has a slightly clunky name — table-driven testing, also called parameterized testing. But the idea behind the name is simple and humane. When the same rule has to hold across many different pairings of input and expected answer, you don't write a separate test for each pair. You write one test, and you hand it a list — a table — of cases. Each row in that table is a little pairing: here's an input, and here's the answer I expect from it. The test then runs itself once for every row, automatically. And here's the part that matters most: it reports each row separately. So if fifteen rows pass and one row fails, you don't get a vague "something's broken." You get a precise finger pointed at the exact row that failed, with the exact input that broke it. One test, many cases, individual verdicts. It's the cure for copy-paste, and it turns a wall of duplication into a single readable list you can scan in seconds.

Let me ground that in something real inside Echo. The app can read books aloud in a synthetic voice, and before it does, it has to clean up the raw text so the voice pronounces things the way a human would. That cleanup lives in a part called the Text Normalizer — "normalize" just means "rewrite into the standard, speakable form." And the way you'd never want to test that is by writing one separate test per transformation. Instead, there's a single table-driven test, and you feed it pairs of before and after.
